source.title: Serotonin and Dopamine: Unifying Affective, Activational, and Decision Functions

confidence_rationale: Proposes that tonic serotonin reflects average punishment rate, analogous to dopamine reflecting average reward rate. This is the most directly testable formulation of the tonic floor concept relevant to INV-052: in harm-rich environments where punishment rate is high, tonic 5-HT should modulate sustained goal-directed behaviour. The paper provides the theoretical bridge between average punishment rate and behavioural vigour that MECH-187 (transduction gain) requires.
failure_signatures: When punishment rate rises, tonic serotonin inhibits vigour -- this is the opposite direction from benefit orientation maintenance, suggesting tonic 5-HT may suppress rather than support goal pursuit in high-harm environments

The average-punishment-rate framing predicts that high tonic 5-HT in chronic harm environments should reduce approach -- which partially challenges MECH-187's claim that tonic 5-HT maintains transduction gain

mapping.source_claim_statement: Tonic serotonin reports average punishment rates; when actions are more likely to have aversive outcomes, vigorous action is more costly, so serotonin inhibits behavioural activation.
mapping.ree_translation: This is a partial support / partial challenge for INV-052. The 'average punishment rate' formulation supports the claim that a tonic regulatory system tracks the harm environment (terrain stage, MECH-186). However, the predicted response is inhibition of vigour, not maintenance of benefit orientation. MECH-187 (transduction gain maintenance) requires the tonic system to preserve seeding gain -- but Cools et al. predict that higher tonic 5-HT in high-harm environments would reduce this gain. INV-052 therefore requires an additional mechanism: the tonic floor must not simply reflect average punishment but must also decouple terrain encoding from invigoration suppression.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The inhibition-of-vigour prediction from Cools et al. creates a direct tension with MECH-187. REE resolves this by distinguishing terrain representation (where benefit is) from invigoration (how hard to pursue it). Cools et al. do not make this distinction. The mapping is therefore partial -- supportive for terrain, challenging for transduction.
mapping.source_context: Theoretical review drawing on primate electrophysiology (DRN firing rates), rodent pharmacology, and human decision-making tasks. Average-rate hypothesis is the core theoretical contribution.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Neuropsychopharmacology, well-cited). Mapping fidelity reduced because the average-punishment-rate prediction creates a directional tension with MECH-187 that INV-052 must explicitly address. This is the most important paper for identifying the boundary conditions of the claim.
